relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Nicodemus comes as a ruler and seeker.
He comes by night, recognizing signs but not yet seeing fully.
Jesus speaks of birth from above/anew as the way to see the Divine reign.
Nicodemus hears only ordinary birth and asks how it can happen.
Water and Spirit open entrance into the reign.
Spirit-born life is not reducible to ordinary origin.
The birth from above is necessary, not optional.
Spirit is like wind: heard, real, free, unpossessable.
Nicodemus remains in the question.
The teacher of Israel is invited into deeper knowing.
Witness speaks from seeing, yet is not received.
Earthly and heavenly trust are linked.
The Son of Humanity bridges heaven and earth.
As Moses lifted the serpent, the Son of Humanity must be lifted.
Trust opens age-long life.
The Divine loves the world by giving the Son for life rather than ruin.
The sending is for saving/healing the world, not condemning it.
Trust moves one out of judgment; refusal remains in self-judgment.
Light comes, and darkness is chosen when works fear exposure.
Harmful practice avoids the light.
Truth-doing comes to the light because its works are in the Divine.
Jesus remains with disciples in Judea and baptizes.
John continues baptizing where much water is present.
John’s imprisonment has not yet come.
Purification becomes a point of discussion.
John’s disciples notice people going to Jesus.
John receives his place as gift from heaven.
He repeats: I am not Messiah; I am sent before him.
The friend of the bridegroom rejoices at the bridegroom’s voice.
His increase is John’s fulfilled joy.
The one from above speaks from above; earth speaks from earth.
He witnesses what he has seen and heard.
Receiving the witness seals the Divine as true.
The sent one speaks Divine words and gives Spirit without measure.
The Father loves the Son and places all in his hand.
Trust in the Son participates in age-long life; refusal remains outside that life.
